sectionName: Chemical & Physical Foundations of Biological Systems

questions.passage:
A protein chemist purifies enzyme E from a cell lysate. At pH 7.4, enzyme E has net negative charge and binds an anion-exchange resin. A linear NaCl gradient elutes bound proteins while fractions are monitored for total protein by A280 and for enzyme activity. The resulting elution profile is shown in Figure 1.



The active fraction is then loaded onto a size-exclusion column. Calibration proteins show that larger native complexes elute earlier because they enter fewer pores. SDS-PAGE under reducing conditions gives a single 50 kDa band, while native PAGE estimates a 100 kDa complex.
questions.stem: Why does enzyme E bind the anion-exchange resin at pH 7.4?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It has net positive charge and binds a negative resin

choices.label: B
choices.text: It has net negative charge and binds a positively charged stationary phase

choices.label: C
choices.text: It is larger than all other proteins

explanations.A: Anion-exchange resins carry a positive charge and bind negatively charged molecules. A protein with net positive charge would be repelled by the positively charged resin and would flow through without binding. At pH 7.4 (above the enzyme's pI), the enzyme carries a net negative charge, enabling electrostatic binding to the cationic resin matrix.
explanations.B: Correct. Anion exchange retains negatively charged molecules on positively charged groups. Because enzyme E is net negative at pH 7.4, it binds until salt ions compete for electrostatic interactions. The method separates primarily by charge, not by size or catalytic activity itself.
explanations.C: Protein size does not explain binding to an ion-exchange resin. Ion-exchange chromatography separates molecules primarily by charge, while size is tested later by size-exclusion chromatography and PAGE. A large protein would not necessarily bind unless it carried the appropriate net charge at the running pH.
explanations.D: All proteins contain peptide bonds — they are polymers of amino acids linked by peptide (amide) bonds. The absence of peptide bonds would mean the molecule is not a protein at all. Binding to ion-exchange resin depends on the protein's net surface charge at the running pH, not on whether it contains peptide bonds.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 1. Anion-exchange chromatography elution profile showing A₂₈₀, enzyme activity, and NaCl gradient.

questions.stem: Which region of Figure 1 is most enriched for enzyme E?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The middle activity peak where enzyme activity is high relative to total protein

choices.label: B
choices.text: The early high-A280 peak with low activity

choices.label: C
choices.text: The late shoulder where both activity and A280 are low

explanations.A: Correct. The middle region has the highest enzyme activity relative to total protein signal, indicating greater specific activity. Purification is judged by activity per amount of protein, not simply the largest A280 peak. A large total-protein peak can contain abundant contaminants with little enzyme E.
explanations.B: The early high-A₂₈₀ peak with minimal enzyme activity represents proteins that do not bind tightly to the resin and elute at low salt. These are contaminant proteins with little or no enzyme E. Specific activity (activity per total protein) is low in this fraction because the protein is abundant but the target enzyme is absent or scarce.
explanations.C: The late shoulder where both activity and A₂₈₀ are low contains little enzyme and little total protein. While the ratio might not be terrible, the absolute amount of enzyme is negligible. Enrichment is best judged by the fraction where enzyme activity peaks relative to total protein — that is the middle peak, where specific activity is highest.
explanations.D: The regions are not equally enriched. Enrichment depends on specific activity — enzyme activity relative to total protein. A region with high A280 but low activity contains mostly contaminating protein, while the middle activity peak has the strongest evidence for enzyme E enrichment.

questions.stem: Why does increasing salt elute proteins from ion-exchange resin?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Salt ions compete for charged interactions with the resin

explanations.A: Salt does not make proteins radioactive. Radioactivity requires unstable nuclei or radiolabeling, neither of which is part of ordinary ion-exchange elution. Salt elutes proteins by competing with charged protein groups for interactions with the resin.
explanations.B: Salt does not break every peptide bond under normal chromatography conditions. Peptide-bond hydrolysis requires strong acid/base, enzymes, or harsh chemical conditions. A NaCl gradient is used to weaken electrostatic binding, not to digest proteins.
explanations.C: Correct. Salt shields and competes for electrostatic interactions between charged proteins and the stationary phase. Proteins with weaker interactions elute at lower salt, while more strongly bound proteins require higher salt. The protein is not chemically converted or digested by salt under ordinary conditions.
explanations.D: Salt does not convert proteins into DNA. Proteins and DNA are chemically distinct biopolymers: proteins are amino acid chains with peptide bonds, while DNA is a nucleotide polymer with phosphodiester bonds. Increasing ionic strength during salt gradient elution displaces proteins from the resin by competing for electrostatic binding sites — it does not alter molecular identity.

questions.stem: What does SDS-PAGE showing one 50 kDa band while native PAGE suggests 100 kDa imply?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The enzyme has no quaternary structure

choices.label: B
choices.text: The enzyme is a 500 bp DNA fragment

choices.label: C
choices.text: SDS-PAGE measures only charge and not mass

choices.label: D
choices.text: The native enzyme is likely a homodimer of two 50 kDa subunits

explanations.A: The data directly indicate quaternary structure. SDS-PAGE denatures the protein and separates individual polypeptides by mass (50 kDa subunit), while native PAGE preserves the intact complex (100 kDa). The 2:1 ratio of native to denatured mass strongly suggests a homodimer. Saying the enzyme has no quaternary structure contradicts the discrepancy between the two techniques.
explanations.B: The units and molecule type are wrong. A 500 bp value describes a DNA fragment length in base pairs, whereas SDS-PAGE and native PAGE here measure protein mass in kilodaltons. The data indicate a 50 kDa polypeptide subunit forming a 100 kDa native protein complex, not a DNA fragment.
explanations.C: SDS-PAGE separates proteins by molecular mass, not charge. SDS binds uniformly to denatured polypeptides (~1.4 g SDS per g protein), masking intrinsic charge differences and giving all proteins a similar charge-to-mass ratio. Migration then depends almost entirely on size, with smaller polypeptides migrating faster through the polyacrylamide sieve.
explanations.D: Correct. SDS and reducing conditions disrupt noncovalent interactions and often disulfides, revealing subunit mass. A single 50 kDa band plus a 100 kDa native complex suggests two identical subunits. Native PAGE preserves quaternary structure more than SDS-PAGE.

questions.stem: In size-exclusion chromatography, which species elutes first?

explanations.A: Correct. Large complexes enter fewer beads pores and take a shorter path, so they elute earlier. Smaller molecules penetrate more pores and are delayed. Charge may matter in other chromatography modes, but size-exclusion is primarily based on hydrodynamic radius.
explanations.B: In size-exclusion chromatography, the smallest molecules elute last, not first. Small molecules enter the pores of the gel beads and take a longer path through the column. Large molecules are excluded from the pores and pass through the interstitial space quickly, eluting first. Elution order is inversely related to molecular size.
explanations.C: Size-exclusion chromatography separates by size (hydrodynamic radius), not by charge. There is no electrostatic interaction between proteins and the inert gel matrix (e.g., Sephadex, Sephacryl). The most negatively charged protein has no advantage in this technique — only its physical size determines when it exits the column.
explanations.D: Enzyme activity does not determine elution order in size-exclusion chromatography. The separation is purely physical — based on molecular dimensions relative to the gel's pore size. A small, highly active enzyme elutes later than a large, inactive complex. Catalytic properties are measured after fractionation, not during the separation.

questions.passage: A second experiment separates DNA fragments by agarose gel electrophoresis. Samples are loaded near the negative electrode and migrate toward the positive electrode because the phosphate backbone is negatively charged. A plasmid digest produces fragments of 500 bp, 1500 bp, and 3000 bp. The researchers compare band intensity, migration distance, and restriction-map predictions. They note that supercoiled uncut plasmid can migrate anomalously compared with linear DNA markers.
questions.stem: Why do DNA fragments migrate toward the positive electrode?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Agarose gives DNA a positive charge

choices.label: B
choices.text: Their phosphate backbone gives them net negative charge

choices.label: D
choices.text: DNA has no charge but follows water flow

explanations.A: Agarose is an electrically neutral polysaccharide matrix — it does not confer charge on DNA. DNA's negative charge is intrinsic, arising from the ionized phosphodiester backbone (one negative charge per nucleotide at neutral pH). The agarose gel provides a sieving medium for size-based separation, while DNA's own charge drives its electrophoretic migration.
explanations.B: Correct. DNA has a negatively charged phosphate backbone, so an electric field drives fragments toward the anode. Agarose acts as a molecular sieve, separating fragments mostly by size. The direction of migration follows charge, while distance depends strongly on length and conformation.
explanations.C: DNA fragments are not magnetic. Their migration in electrophoresis is driven by their intrinsic negative charge interacting with the applied electric field, not by any magnetic property. Magnetism involves response to magnetic fields through unpaired electrons or ferromagnetic domains — none of which apply to nucleic acids under normal conditions.
explanations.D: DNA carries substantial negative charge from its phosphodiester backbone — approximately one negative charge per nucleotide. This charge is what drives electrophoretic migration toward the anode (positive electrode). DNA does not follow passive water flow (electroosmosis); its movement is an active electrophoretic response to the electric field acting on its charged backbone.

questions.stem: Which linear DNA fragment should migrate farthest in agarose?

explanations.A: Linear DNA fragments do not all migrate equally in agarose. The gel matrix separates them by size: smaller fragments encounter less resistance and travel farther, while larger fragments remain closer to the wells.
explanations.B: A 1500 bp fragment is smaller than 3000 bp, but it is still larger than 500 bp. Since smaller linear DNA fragments migrate farther, 1500 bp would travel an intermediate distance, not the farthest.
explanations.C: A 3000 bp fragment is the largest option, so it would migrate the shortest distance among the listed linear fragments. Larger DNA has more difficulty moving through the agarose pores and stays closer to the wells.
explanations.D: Correct. Smaller linear DNA fragments move more easily through the agarose matrix and therefore migrate farther in a given time. Larger fragments experience more resistance and remain closer to the wells. This size-based behavior assumes comparable linear conformation.

questions.stem: Why can uncut supercoiled plasmid migrate anomalously?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Shape and topology affect mobility, so it may not track with linear size markers

choices.label: D
choices.text: It always migrates exactly as a linear molecule of equal base pairs

explanations.A: Supercoiled plasmid DNA does enter agarose gels — it migrates and produces a visible band. Its compact supercoiled topology actually allows it to move more easily through the gel pores than relaxed circular forms in many conditions. The anomaly is not failure to enter but unexpected migration position relative to linear markers of the same molecular weight.
explanations.B: All DNA molecules, including plasmids, contain a sugar-phosphate backbone with phosphodiester bonds. The phosphate groups are ionized at neutral pH, giving DNA its characteristic negative charge. Supercoiled plasmid DNA has the same chemical backbone as linear DNA — its anomalous migration is due to its compact 3D topology, not any absence of charged groups.
explanations.C: Correct. Supercoiling changes compactness and friction through the gel, so mobility may differ from linear DNA of the same length. Linear ladders are calibrated for linear fragments. This is why restriction digestion is often used to interpret plasmid size and map structure.
explanations.D: An uncut supercoiled plasmid does not always migrate like a linear molecule of the same base-pair length. Supercoiling makes the molecule more compact, changing its friction and movement through the gel. That is exactly why linear size markers can mislead when interpreting uncut plasmids.

questions.stem: If a digest produces 500, 1500, and 3000 bp fragments, what was the plasmid size?

explanations.A: 1500 bp is only one of the restriction fragments, not the total plasmid size. In a complete digest of a circular plasmid, all fragment lengths must be added to recover the original plasmid length.
explanations.B: Correct. Restriction fragments from a complete digest sum to the original circular plasmid length: 500 + 1500 + 3000 = 5000 bp. The largest band alone is not the plasmid size when multiple fragments are generated by restriction cuts.
explanations.C: 4500 bp comes from leaving out the 500 bp fragment. Complete restriction-digest fragments are additive, so every observed fragment must be included: 500 + 1500 + 3000 = 5000 bp.
explanations.D: 3000 bp is the largest fragment, but the largest band is not the plasmid size when multiple fragments are produced. The plasmid size is the sum of all complete-digest fragments, which is 5000 bp.

questions.stem: What does a brighter band usually indicate when staining is proportional to DNA mass?

choices.label: A
choices.text: More DNA mass in that band

explanations.A: Correct. Band intensity generally reflects the amount of DNA stain bound, which scales with DNA mass if staining and imaging are in the linear range. A larger fragment can appear brighter than an equal molar amount of a smaller fragment because it contains more base pairs.
explanations.B: Band brightness in DNA gels stained with intercalating dyes (ethidium bromide, SYBR) reflects the amount of DNA, not the fragment's charge. All DNA fragments have essentially the same charge-to-mass ratio (one phosphate per nucleotide), so charge does not vary meaningfully between fragments. A brighter band simply means more DNA mass — either from a larger fragment or more copies of a smaller one.
explanations.C: All DNA fragments consist of nucleotide bases joined by a sugar-phosphate backbone. A fragment with 'no bases' would not be DNA — it would have no nitrogenous bases to intercalate dye and would not produce a visible band. Band brightness correlates with DNA mass available to bind the fluorescent stain, not with the absence of molecular components.
explanations.D: Brighter bands in DNA electrophoresis represent more DNA, not protein contamination. Protein contaminants typically do not intercalate ethidium bromide or SYBR dyes (which bind specifically to nucleic acid structure) and would not produce bright fluorescent bands. Protein contamination is assessed by separate methods like A₂₆₀/A₂₈₀ ratio or Bradford assay.
